title: Rewrite My Draft to Be Clear, Friendly, and Skimmable (Keep Meaning Intact)
content:
Rewrite the text below so it’s clear, friendly, and easy to skim.

Rules:
- Keep the meaning and key details exactly the same (don’t add new claims).
- Use plain English and short sentences.
- If something is ambiguous, keep it neutral rather than guessing.
- Preserve names, dates, numbers, links, and any requirements.
- Improve flow with light structure (short paragraphs + optional bullets).
- Remove fluff, repetition, and passive voice where possible.

Output:
1) Revised version
2) 3–5 suggested subject lines / titles (if relevant)
3) A short list of any unclear parts you noticed (as questions)

Text to rewrite:
[PASTE HERE]

title: Turn Product Features Into Benefit-First Marketing Copy (Landing + Ads)
content:
I’m going to paste product info. Turn it into benefit-first marketing copy that’s ready to use.

Input (paste below):
- Product name:
- Target customer (who they are + what they care about):
- 3–7 key features (bullets):
- Main problem it solves:
- Proof (numbers, testimonials, logos, guarantees) if any:
- Price/plan notes (optional):
- Primary CTA (e.g., Start free trial, Book a demo):
- Brand voice (e.g., bold, friendly, premium):
- Competitors / alternatives (optional):

Output (keep it concise and structured):
1) One-sentence positioning statement (clear, specific)
2) Landing page hero section:
   - Headline (max 10 words)
   - Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
   - 3 benefit bullets (not features)
   - CTA button text + one microcopy line under the button
3) Social proof block: 2 short lines (use provided proof; if none, write “proof placeholders” without inventing facts)
4) 3 ad variations for paid social:
   - Primary text (max 25 words)
   - Headline (max 6 words)
   - CTA
   - Each variation should focus on a different angle (speed, savings, simplicity, results, risk-free, etc.)

Rules:
- No buzzword fluff. Write like a real brand.
- Don’t invent stats or customer names.
- Prefer concrete outcomes over vague claims.
- If the input is missing something critical, make the best assumption and list it under “Assumptions” at the end (max 3).

title: Minimalist social media product teaser (bold type + clean studio lighting)
content:
Create a modern social media teaser image for a new product launch.

Subject: a sleek matte-black wireless earbud case (closed) with a subtle embossed logo (no readable brand text).
Composition: square 1:1, centered product on a smooth gradient backdrop (deep charcoal to soft graphite), generous negative space. Add one bold headline in the top-left: “DROP 01” (simple sans-serif, high contrast, clean kerning). Add a small subline beneath it: “Available Friday” (smaller weight). Keep text minimal and crisp.
Lighting: soft studio key light from upper right, gentle rim light on left edge, natural shadow under the case, mild specular highlights.
Style: high-end minimalist product photography, modern and direct, no clutter, no extra props.
Color palette: monochrome with a single accent color (electric cyan) used only as a thin underline beneath “DROP 01”.
Camera: 50mm look, slight top-down angle (~10–15°), sharp focus on product, clean edges.
Output: ultra-detailed, realistic, social-feed ready, no watermark, no mock UI, no additional text beyond the two lines specified.

title: Turn rough notes into a clear, action-oriented email (with subject options)
content:
Rewrite my notes into a concise, practical email that’s easy to act on.

Context/notes (verbatim):
[PASTE NOTES]

Audience:
- Recipient: [name/role]
- Relationship: [client/teammate/vendor/etc.]
- Goal of the email: [request/decision/update/follow-up]

Constraints:
- Length: 120–200 words unless my notes require more
- Tone: clear, practical, calm
- Avoid fluff, clichés, and excessive apologies
- Use plain language; keep sentences short

Deliver:
1) 6 subject line options (mix of straightforward + slightly more specific)
2) The email body with:
   - One-sentence opener that states why I’m writing
   - 2–4 tight bullet points for key info
   - A direct ask with a deadline/date if applicable
   - A short closing line
3) If anything is missing or ambiguous, list up to 3 targeted questions at the end (don’t block the draft).

Optional:
- Provide a second version that’s 30% shorter.
- If my notes include sensitive/negative info, keep the wording neutral and professional.

title: Write a 1-page business case for a proposed initiative (clear ROI, risks, next steps)
content:
Write a one-page business case for the initiative described below. Keep it professional, concise, and easy for executives to scan.

Initiative:
- Name:
- Problem/opportunity (current state + impact):
- Proposed solution (what will change):
- Target customers/users:
- Strategic alignment (company goals this supports):
- Timeline (start date, milestones, go-live):
- Estimated costs (one-time, recurring):
- Expected benefits (revenue, savings, risk reduction, customer impact):
- Key assumptions:
- Constraints/dependencies:
- Main risks + mitigations:
- Alternatives considered (and why not chosen):
- Decision needed from leadership:

Output format (use these headings exactly):
1) Executive summary (3–4 sentences)
2) Why now (2–3 bullets)
3) Proposal (4–6 bullets)
4) Cost & resourcing (table: item | amount | notes)
5) Benefits & ROI (table: benefit | metric | estimate | timing)
6) Risks & mitigations (table: risk | likelihood | impact | mitigation)
7) Success metrics (5 measurable KPIs)
8) Recommendation & next steps (3 bullets with owners)

Rules:
- Use plain language; avoid jargon.
- Include realistic ranges if numbers are uncertain (e.g., "$80–120k").
- If any input is missing, state it as an assumption rather than asking questions.

title: Turn a Rough Product Idea Into a Simple UI Concept + Key Screens
content:
Help me turn this product idea into a clear, simple UI concept.

Product idea (rough notes):
[Paste notes here]

Constraints:
- Target users: [who]
- Platform: [web / iOS / Android / desktop]
- Primary goal (1 sentence): [goal]
- Must-have features: [list]
- Nice-to-haves: [list]
- Brand vibe (3–5 words): [e.g., calm, playful, premium]

What I want from you (keep it lightweight):
1) A one-paragraph product pitch (plain language, no jargon).
2) The core user flow in 5–8 steps.
3) A list of the 3–5 most important screens, each with:
   - Screen name
   - Purpose
   - Key UI elements (bullets)
   - Primary CTA
4) A quick navigation structure (e.g., tabs, sidebar, or single-flow) and why it fits.
5) 3 UX risks or confusing spots you anticipate, with a fix for each.

Guidelines:
- Prioritize clarity and speed over fancy features.
- If my notes conflict, pick the simplest assumption and state it.
- Use short bullets; avoid long explanations.

title: Cinematic Portrait Prompt Builder (subject + lighting + lens + mood)
content:
Create an image-generation prompt for a single portrait. Ask me for only 5 inputs (and wait for my answers):
1) Subject (who/what) + 1 defining detail
2) Setting (location + time of day)
3) Mood (3 adjectives)
4) Style target (e.g., film still, editorial, painterly, anime, 3D)
5) Output format (aspect ratio + any must-avoid items)

Then produce:
A) One final prompt (70–120 words) that specifies composition (framing, pose, camera angle), lighting (key/fill/rim or natural), lens + camera settings (focal length, aperture, ISO), color palette, textures/materials, and background details.
B) A short “negative prompt” line focused on common failures (extra limbs, deformed hands, warped face, messy text, watermark, low-res, oversharpening).

Constraints:
- Be concrete and visual; no vague adjectives without a supporting detail.
- Keep it to one character unless I explicitly ask for more.
- If my inputs conflict (e.g., ‘noir’ + ‘pastel’), pick the best interpretation and state the choice in one sentence before the prompt.

title: Modern coding workspace hero image (no cover text, no overlay, no extra padding)
content:
Create a modern, direct “coding” hero image with NO title text and NO UI overlay. Scene: a clean desk setup in a dim studio at night, single ultrawide monitor showing a code editor with a readable-but-generic code layout (no real company names, no copyrighted logos), terminal pane with subtle build output, and a small git diff panel. Include a mechanical keyboard (matte black), a minimal mouse, a ceramic mug, and a few neatly routed cables.

Composition: 16:9, centered monitor, slight 3/4 angle from the right, camera at desk height, shallow depth of field (sharp on the monitor and keyboard, soft falloff to background). Lighting: moody teal-and-amber practical lighting, soft key light from monitor glow, gentle rim light on keyboard edges, realistic reflections on screen. Background: tasteful blurred shelves with a couple of books and a small plant, no readable book titles.

Style: photorealistic, modern tech aesthetic, clean surfaces, no clutter. Color palette: charcoal, slate, teal accents, warm amber highlights. Constraints: no text overlays, no watermark, no frame, no extra border/padding, no “cover” layout space, avoid exaggerated cyberpunk elements.

title: Turn a messy to-do list into a realistic daily plan (with time blocks)
content:
Take my raw to-do list and turn it into a realistic plan for today.

Input:
- My working hours today: [e.g., 9:30–5:30]
- Fixed commitments (meetings/appointments): [list + times]
- My to-do list (messy is fine):
[paste here]
- Energy level today (low/medium/high): [ ]
- Hard constraints (breaks, pick-up times, no deep work after 3pm, etc.): [ ]

What I want from you:
1) Clarify duplicates/unclear items by making smart assumptions (don’t ask more than 3 questions). If something is too vague, rewrite it into a concrete next action.
2) Estimate effort for each task (S=5–15 min, M=15–45 min, L=45–90 min). If you’re unsure, pick a conservative estimate.
3) Choose the 3 most important outcomes for today and explain in one line why each made the cut.
4) Produce a time-block schedule that fits my hours and commitments:
   - Include breaks and admin buffer time.
   - Put deep work earlier if possible.
   - Batch small tasks.
   - If the list doesn’t fit, push overflow to a “Later” section.
5) Output format:
   - “Top 3 outcomes”
   - “Schedule” (timeline with start/end times)
   - “Later (not today)”
   - “If I finish early” (2–4 optional quick wins)

Keep it clear and practical. No motivational speech.

title: High-Confidence Research Brief with Claims, Evidence Grading, and Open Questions
content:
Act as a research analyst. I’m going to give you a topic and (optionally) a few starting links/notes. Produce a decision-ready research brief that prioritizes accuracy and clearly separates verified facts from hypotheses.

Topic: [paste topic]
Audience/role: [e.g., product lead, policy team, investor]
Scope boundaries (include/exclude): [paste]
Time horizon: [e.g., last 3 years, historical]
Geography/market: [paste]
Constraints: [time, word count, must-use sources, etc.]

Deliverable requirements (follow this structure):
1) Executive answer (5–8 bullets)
   - The “so what” conclusions first.
   - Include 1–2 quantified statements where possible.

2) Key questions and assumptions
   - List the 6–10 questions that drive the analysis.
   - State your assumptions explicitly; label each as “critical” or “non-critical.”

3) Claims table (core of the brief)
   - Create a table with columns:
     Claim | Status (Supported / Mixed / Unclear / Likely false) | Evidence summary | Best sources to verify | Confidence (0–100) | Notes/limitations
   - Include 10–20 claims, starting with the most decision-relevant.

4) Evidence map
   - Group evidence by type: peer-reviewed research, reputable journalism, government/IGO, industry reports, company disclosures, expert commentary.
   - For each group, explain typical strengths/biases and how you weighted it.

5) What experts disagree on
   - 3–7 areas of active debate.
   - For each: the competing views, what would change your mind, and what data would resolve it.

6) Risks, second-order effects, and counterarguments
   - Provide at least 8 items.
   - Include at least 2 “contrarian” points that challenge the main narrative.

7) Practical implications and options
   - 3–5 options/paths forward relevant to the audience.
   - For each: expected upside, downside, prerequisites, and leading indicators to monitor.

8) Research gaps + next-step plan
   - A prioritized list of missing info.
   - A 2-hour, 2-day, and 2-week research plan with concrete actions.

Sourcing and rigor rules:
- If you’re not certain, say so and explain why.
- Avoid “citation theater.” Prefer fewer, stronger sources over many weak ones.
- When mentioning a statistic, specify: who measured it, when, sample/coverage (if known), and any caveats.
- Do not fabricate citations. If browsing isn’t available, write “Needs verification” and propose exactly where to check.

Output style:
- Professional, concise, and skimmable.
- Use tables where helpful.
- Minimize jargon; define necessary terms in-line.

Before you start, ask up to 5 targeted clarifying questions only if they materially change the outcome. Otherwise proceed with stated assumptions and clearly label them.

title: Turn a messy meeting recap into clear action items, owners, and deadlines (business-ready)
content:
I’m going to paste a rough meeting recap with scattered notes. Convert it into a clean, business-ready output that a team can act on.

Requirements:
- Tone: friendly and helpful, but concise.
- Do NOT invent facts. If something is missing, write “TBD” and list it under “Open Questions”.
- Keep it skimmable and formatted with headings and bullet points.

Output format:
1) One-sentence meeting purpose
2) Key decisions (bullets)
3) Action items (table with: Task | Owner | Due date | Priority | Notes)
4) Risks / blockers (bullets)
5) Open questions (bullets)
6) Next meeting (proposed agenda bullets + who should attend)

Here are the notes:
[PASTE MEETING NOTES HERE]

title: Turn Rough Notes Into a Clear, Actionable One-Page Brief
content:
Convert my rough notes into a one-page brief that someone can act on immediately.

What I’ll paste:
- Audience:
- Goal / decision needed:
- Context (optional):
- Notes (bullets, messy is fine):

Your output (keep it under ~500 words):
1) Headline: a single sentence that states the core point
2) What’s happening: 3–5 tight bullets capturing the situation
3) Key insights: 3 bullets that explain the “so what” (no repeats)
4) Recommendation: one clear recommended path, plus 1 alternative option
5) Risks & mitigations: 3 rows in a small table (Risk | Impact | Mitigation)
6) Next steps: 5 checklist items with an owner role (e.g., Marketing, Eng, Ops) and a suggested timeframe

Rules:
- Preserve all important facts; don’t invent specifics. If something is missing, add a short “Open question:” bullet instead.
- Use concrete language, active verbs, and consistent terms.
- If the notes contain conflicting info, flag it in “Open questions” and propose how to resolve.
- Write in a confident, structured tone—no fluff.

Start by asking up to 3 clarifying questions only if absolutely necessary; otherwise produce the brief immediately.

Here are my notes:
[PASTE]

title: Modern product launch hero image for a SaaS landing page (clean, high-contrast)
content:
Create a modern marketing hero image for a SaaS product launch. Subject: a sleek, abstract “control center” UI floating in 3D space (no readable text), composed of modular cards, charts, toggles, and a subtle network graph. Style: clean, minimal, high-contrast, modern and direct. Color palette: deep charcoal background with electric cyan and soft violet accents, small warm white highlights.

Composition: wide 16:9, lots of negative space on the left for headline placement, the UI cluster sits on the right third; add a faint perspective grid and soft volumetric haze for depth. Lighting: crisp rim light, gentle top light, soft reflections, subtle bloom on accent elements; shadows are soft and controlled.

Materials: glassmorphism panels with frosted edges, anodized metal frames, thin glowing lines, micro-texture grain. Mood: confident, premium, tech-forward, not playful.

Camera: 35mm equivalent, slightly above eye level, mild depth of field (keep main UI sharp). Render: ultra-clean, high resolution, realistic 3D/CGI (Octane/Redshift look), no people, no logos, no text, no watermark, no clutter.

title: Turn messy notes into a clear, action-oriented email (with subject + CTA)
content:
Write a clear, practical email based on the notes I paste below.

What I’ll provide:
- Audience/recipient:
- My role (optional):
- Goal of the email (what I want the reader to do/decide):
- Context/background (bullets or rough text):
- Key points to include (bullets):
- Constraints (word limit, tone, must-mention items, dates, links):
- Call to action + deadline (if any):

Your output:
1) 3 subject line options (one straightforward, one concise, one specific)
2) The email body in plain text
   - Start with 1–2 sentences of context
   - Use short paragraphs and bullets where helpful
   - Make asks and next steps unambiguous
   - Include any dates/times in a consistent format
3) A “Next steps” section with 2–5 bullet points
4) If anything is unclear, list up to 3 assumptions you made (don’t ask me questions unless essential)

Style rules:
- Keep it skimmable and under the word limit if provided
- Avoid fluff, buzzwords, and over-apologizing
- Use simple language and active voice
- If the notes are long, prioritize what directly supports the goal and move the rest into a short “Additional details” section.

Notes to convert into an email:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: Write a clear design brief from messy project notes
content:
Turn my rough notes into a one-page design brief a designer can start from immediately.

Input (paste below):
- Project/background:
- Target audience:
- Problem to solve:
- Key messages:
- Brand/product context (links, competitors, existing assets):
- Deliverables needed:
- Must-haves / constraints (legal, accessibility, platforms, sizes):
- Nice-to-haves:
- Timeline and milestones:
- Stakeholders/approvers:
- Success criteria:

Output requirements:
- Keep it professional and concise.
- Use headings and tight bullet points.
- Flag assumptions and risks.
- List exactly 5 open questions that would unblock the work.
- Include a “Scope boundaries” section (what’s explicitly out of scope).

My notes:
[PASTE HERE]

title: Rewrite My Draft to Be Clear, Friendly, and Skimmable (Keep My Meaning)
content:
Rewrite the text below so it’s clearer, friendlier, and easier to skim—without changing the meaning or adding new claims.

What I’m sending this for: [email / Slack / doc / support reply / announcement]
Audience: [who it’s for]
Desired tone: friendly, helpful, confident (not salesy)
Length target: [short / medium / keep similar]
Call to action (if any): [what you want them to do]

Text to rewrite:
---
[paste draft here]
---

Constraints:
- Keep all factual details (dates, numbers, names). If something is ambiguous, flag it instead of guessing.
- Preserve my intent, but remove fluff, repeated phrases, and overly formal wording.
- Prefer active voice and simple verbs.
- Use short paragraphs and add bullets where it improves readability.
- If there’s a sensitive point (delay, mistake, refusal), state it directly but tactfully.

Output:
1) Rewritten version
2) 3–5 suggested subject lines / opening lines (if relevant)
3) A quick checklist of anything unclear or missing that I should confirm

title: Launch Email + Landing Page Copy for a New Feature (Benefit-First)
content:
Write benefit-first marketing copy for launching a new feature. Keep it confident, structured, and easy to skim.

Context
- Product/company: [WHAT YOU SELL]
- Audience: [WHO IT’S FOR]
- New feature: [FEATURE NAME + 1-SENTENCE DESCRIPTION]
- Primary customer pain it solves: [PAIN]
- Key benefits (3): [BENEFIT 1], [BENEFIT 2], [BENEFIT 3]
- Proof/credibility: [METRIC/QUOTE/LOGO/RESULT]
- CTA: [START TRIAL / BOOK DEMO / UPGRADE / BUY]
- Offer or urgency (optional): [DEADLINE / BONUS]
- Compliance notes (optional): [RESTRICTIONS]

Deliverables
1) Subject lines (6)
- 2 curiosity-driven
- 2 benefit-driven
- 2 short (under 35 characters)

2) Launch email (one email)
- 1 strong opening line that names the pain
- 3 short sections with headings (one per key benefit)
- One sentence of proof placed after the second section
- Clear CTA button copy + a fallback text link
- P.S. that reiterates the main outcome + urgency/offer if provided
- Max 180 words, no jargon

3) Landing page copy (above-the-fold only)
- Headline (max 10 words)
- Subheadline (1–2 sentences)
- 5 bullet benefits (each starts with a verb)
- “How it works” in 3 steps (each step max 8 words)
- Social proof line (1 sentence)
- Primary CTA + secondary CTA

Style rules
- Write in second person (“you”)
- Use concrete outcomes (time saved, errors reduced, revenue gained)
- Avoid hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, best-in-class)
- If any input is missing, make a reasonable assumption and note it briefly at the end under “Assumptions.”

title: Modern email workspace hero image (clean, direct, no clutter)
content:
Create a modern, direct hero image for an email product page: a minimalist desk scene with a slim laptop open to a clean email client UI (inbox list on the left, single email open on the right), simple monochrome icons, clear subject lines, and a visible “Send” button. Include subtle emphasis on speed and clarity: one highlighted message thread, a small status chip reading “Scheduled 9:00 AM”, and a tidy search bar at the top.

Style: contemporary tech aesthetic, crisp lines, restrained color palette (soft whites, charcoal, one accent color like cobalt), lots of negative space, no decorative clutter.

Composition: 16:9 wide, camera at a slight 3/4 angle above desk level, laptop centered with generous margin for headline text on the right side of the frame.

Lighting: soft natural window light with gentle shadows, high clarity, no harsh glare on screen.

Details: matte desk surface, one simple mug and a single pen only; no visible brand logos; UI text should be believable but not real company names; avoid unreadable microtext.

Mood: efficient, calm, confident.

Output: photorealistic with subtle CGI polish, ultra-sharp, high resolution.

title: Turn messy notes into an actionable plan (with priorities and next steps)
content:
Take the text I paste below (it may be messy, repetitive, or out of order) and turn it into a clear, practical action plan.

Requirements:
- Keep it short and usable, not essay-like.
- Preserve any concrete facts, dates, numbers, and constraints exactly.
- If something is ambiguous, flag it as an open question instead of guessing.
- Use plain language.

Output format:
1) One-sentence goal
2) Key context (3–6 bullets)
3) Priorities (P0/P1/P2) with 1-line rationale each
4) Action steps (5–12 bullets) written as verbs, each with an owner (if known) and a due date (if mentioned)
5) Risks & blockers (bullets)
6) Open questions (bullets)

Here are the notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: Turn Messy Notes Into a Decision-Ready One-Pager (Options, Risks, Ask)
content:
Act as a senior staff writer for internal business memos. I’m going to paste raw, messy notes. Convert them into a crisp, decision-ready one-pager that an executive can approve in under 3 minutes.

Constraints
- Professional, concise, no hype.
- Use plain language; define acronyms on first use.
- Keep to ~400–650 words unless the content truly demands more.
- Don’t invent facts. If something is missing, call it out under “Open Questions”.
- Preserve numbers, dates, and commitments exactly as given.

Output format (use these headings exactly)
1) Decision Needed (1–2 sentences)
2) Context (why now; 3–5 bullets)
3) Proposal (what will be done; 4–7 bullets)
4) Options Considered (2–4 options, each with Pros / Cons / Cost / Timeline)
5) Risks & Mitigations (table: Risk | Impact | Likelihood | Mitigation | Owner)
6) Dependencies & Assumptions (bullets)
7) Success Metrics (5–8 measurable metrics; include target and time window)
8) Timeline & Milestones (bullets with dates if available)
9) Open Questions (bullets; phrase as answerable questions)
10) Appendix: Source Notes (verbatim, lightly cleaned only for typos)

Process rules
- If the notes include multiple threads, consolidate into one coherent narrative; do not duplicate points.
- Translate vague statements into specific, testable language (e.g., “improve performance” → “reduce p95 latency by X%”), but only when the notes provide enough to do so; otherwise leave as “TBD”.
- Where trade-offs exist, make them explicit and balanced.

Here are the raw notes:
[PASTE NOTES HERE]

title: Turn Product Features Into Benefit-Driven Marketing Copy (Website + Ads)
content:
I’m going to paste product info. Turn it into benefit-driven marketing copy that’s clear, friendly, and easy to scan.

Product info:
- Product name:
- What it does (1–2 sentences):
- Target customer:
- Top 5 features (bullets):
- Key differentiator vs alternatives:
- Price point (optional):
- Proof (reviews, stats, clients) (optional):
- Primary call to action:

Create:
1) A one-line positioning statement (plain language)
2) A homepage hero section: headline (max 8 words) + subheadline (max 25 words) + CTA button text (2 options)
3) Three benefit bullets (not features)
4) A short product description (60–90 words)
5) Five ad headlines (max 30 characters each)
6) Five ad descriptions (max 90 characters each)
7) Three social captions (one punchy, one story-style, one question-led)

Rules:
- Avoid hype and jargon. No exclamation marks.
- Keep the tone warm, confident, and helpful.
- If info is missing, make reasonable assumptions and note them at the end under “Assumptions.”

title: Write a confident, structured email that gets a clear decision
content:
Draft a confident, structured email based on the details below. Keep it concise, action-oriented, and easy to scan. Use short paragraphs and bullets where helpful.

Context
- Recipient (name + role): 
- My role/company: 
- Relationship/history (new contact / existing / stakeholder): 
- Topic in one line: 
- Why this matters now (deadline, risk, opportunity): 

What I need
- The decision I’m asking for (be explicit): 
- Options (if any) + my recommendation: 
- Exact call to action (reply with X / approve Y / choose A or B): 
- Deadline for response: 

Supporting details (only the essentials)
- Key facts (max 3): 
- Constraints (budget, timeline, dependencies): 
- What happens if we don’t decide: 

Preferences
- Tone: confident, structured, respectful
- Length: 120–200 words (unless more is truly required)
- Sign-off style (formal / friendly): 

Output requirements
1) Subject line: 1–2 strong options
2) Email body:
   - Opening sentence that sets context
   - 2–4 bullets with the key info
   - Clear ask + deadline
   - Polite close
3) Add a brief “If they push back” section with 2 short follow-up replies for common objections.

If anything important is missing, ask up to 3 clarifying questions before drafting.

title: Modern business hero image: diverse team in a glass-walled meeting room
content: Create a modern business website hero image showing a diverse team of 5 in a glass-walled conference room, mid-discussion around a clean white table with a laptop, minimal notebooks, and a single carafe of water. Setting: high-rise office with floor-to-ceiling windows, soft city skyline out of focus. Composition: wide horizontal (16:9) with negative space on the left for headline text; subjects positioned on the right third; candid but confident body language (no forced smiles). Lighting: bright natural daylight with soft shadows; subtle rim light on shoulders; neutral color temperature. Styling: contemporary tailored clothing in muted tones (navy, charcoal, beige), no loud patterns, no visible brand logos. Camera: eye-level, 35mm lens look, shallow depth of field (faces sharp, background gently blurred). Mood: focused, decisive, collaborative. Photorealistic, high detail, clean and minimal, no exaggerated lens flare, no text, no watermark.

title: Writing Request Router: classify tasks, ask minimum questions, output next action
content:
Act as my writing-ops router. Your job is to take any incoming writing request and turn it into an actionable workflow, not a polished draft.

Input I’ll paste:
- The user’s request (raw text)
- Any constraints (audience, length, tone, channel, deadline)
- Any source material (notes, links, outline) if available

Your output must be a structured routing package with these sections:

1) Task Classification
- Primary task type: {drafting | rewriting | summarizing | brainstorming | outlining | editing | translation | research-needed | formatting | other}
- Writing asset: {email | memo | blog | script | proposal | resume | cover letter | report | social | documentation | story | other}
- Stage: {discovery | first pass | revision | final polish}
- Risk flags (if any): {legal/medical/financial advice | privacy | plagiarism | sensitive topic | brand risk | none}

2) Missing Information (Minimum Questions)
- Ask only the smallest set of questions required to proceed.
- Cap at 5 questions.
- If you can proceed without asking, write “None required” and state assumptions.

3) Recommended Workflow
Provide a step-by-step plan (5–10 steps) tailored to the classification, including:
- What you will produce at each step (artifact name)
- What you need from me at that step (if anything)
- Quality checks (accuracy, tone, constraints, structure)

4) Output Specification (Machine-usable)
Return a JSON object describing what should be generated next:
{
  "next_action": "ask_questions" | "generate_outline" | "generate_first_draft" | "rewrite" | "edit" | "create_variants" | "extract_requirements" | "refuse_or_safe_complete",
  "deliverables": ["..."],
  "constraints": {
    "audience": "...",
    "tone": "...",
    "length": "...",
    "format": "...",
    "must_include": ["..."],
    "must_avoid": ["..."],
    "deadline": "..."
  },
  "assumptions": ["..."],
  "open_questions": ["..."],
  "acceptance_checks": ["..."]
}

5) Fast Start (Optional)
If it’s reasonable, include a tiny starter artifact (choose one):
- 5-bullet outline, OR
- a section-by-section skeleton with headings, OR
- a rewrite plan with before/after rules
Keep this to under 120 words.

Rules
- Do not write the full final piece unless the JSON next_action says to generate a first draft.
- If the request is unsafe or asks for disallowed content, route to refuse_or_safe_complete and propose a safe alternative deliverable.
- Be clear, practical, and concise.

Now wait for my request and run the router.
